as a

Yeah, so when it comes, this is just organic traffic.

Right now, I think it's too early for us to make traffic projections for LLMs because it's so, they're evolving constantly and how they cite content has changed.
So I can actually show you, this is your LLM referral traffic over the, over the.
The entirety of this year.
And you can see there was a big dip right around, this is actually, this is October, this is August or September.
And you can see that the majority of the traffic that went down, went down due to chat GPT referral traffic going down.
And this is when they changed their algorithm so that they were citing more like Wikipedia.
They've updated it since then.
So now you can, you're already kind of seeing more referral traffic come in from chat GPT.
But this is just an example to show you how, like some of these LLMs can be a little bit volatile.
What we typically see is that LLM referral traffic might consist, maybe like be between three to five percent overall of your entire organic traffic.
But it could be more depending on the size of your content library.
We work with a really big cybersecurity client.
They have a huge content library with.

Thousands of educational blog posts, kind of like what we were publishing, and their report, the landing pages here that are getting traffic from LLMs, it's literally almost all blog posts with their homepage and like select product pages.
So organic content really does get overwhelmingly cited by LLMs, and it's a really important part of LLM visibility.

Right now it accounts for, I think, 3% to 5%, but it really depends on the brand, and it depends on, obviously, more and more people have been using LLMs to research companies, so that, like, I feel like that's something that's going to continue to grow in the future.
So is the optimization, though, primarily for SEO, then, for the most part at this stage?
It's for both.
So good SEO is the foundation of LLM optimization.
Anything, if you go, if you dig, if you want to, if you
Optimize for LLMs and for SEO, you generally follow a group of best practices that are very much the same.
That includes writing clear headlines and content, organizing the content around chunks, which means like each paragraph contains unique information and is a standalone unit of information.
The content also includes like TLDR sections with like a bullet point list at the top.
That's more for LLMs.
We also include like frequently asked question sections where we provide very specific answers to what we assume would be could be prompts that people could be using in LLMs.
So it really is both.
For LLMs, one of the things that's really important is covering clusters of topics comprehensively so that, you know, when somebody asks a question about how can I be more productive at work?

The LLMs can cite a blog post from Read.ai about general workplace productivity, but they can also see, okay, Read.ai has this blog post about like how enterprise search and knowledge management can make people more productive at work.
So it's about kind of creating authority in different topic clusters, which is what we're doing in order to optimize for LLMs.
What, out of curiosity, should we expect from a lag perspective?
Obviously, something's indexed, Google will pick it up.
So the organic traffic, I understand really well.

source on that subject.
